source_text: Native North American Ethnobotany — Moerman Database Supplement
source_language: Multiple indigenous languages (oral tradition); documented in English
translator: AI synthesis from Moerman (1998) Native American Ethnobotany, Hamel & Chiltoskey (1975) Cherokee Plants, Densmore (1928) Uses of Plants by the Chippewa, Gilmore (1919) Uses of Plants by the Indians of the Missouri River Region

results.original_text: [Moerman, D.E. 1998. Native American Ethnobotany. Timber Press.]
results.translation: Daniel Moerman's Native American Ethnobotany database documents 4,029 medicinal plant uses across 291 indigenous North American nations — the most comprehensive documented indigenous pharmacopoeia in the world. Key statistical finding: plants used by more than five independent nations show significantly higher rates of confirmed pharmacological activity in modern research (Moerman, 1996) — validating the archive's convergence methodology. The most widely documented plants across the most nations: yarrow (46 nations), elderberry (27 nations), wild bergamot (21 nations), sage (19 nations), and echinacea (13 nations). These represent the highest-convergence indigenous North American plants.

results.title_inferred: Yarrow — 46 nations, the most widely used North American plant
results.original_text: [Achillea millefolium — documented across 46 independent nations by Moerman]
results.translation: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium) is documented by 46 independent North American nations — more than any other plant in the Moerman database. Applications span: wound healing and hemostasis (most common), fever reduction, respiratory infections, digestive complaints, and ceremonial use. The Navajo, Ojibwe, Cherokee, Cheyenne, and Blackfoot nations — geographically separated by thousands of miles — all independently documented yarrow for wound bleeding. This represents the archive's single highest documented convergence for any one plant: 46 independent North American nations plus Celtic/Norse (Bald's Leechbook) plus Greek/Roman (the genus named for Achilles) — potentially 48+ independent traditions for one therapeutic application.
results.preparation: Fresh leaves packed into wounds to stop bleeding; decoction for fever; steam inhalation for respiratory
results.condition: Wound bleeding (hemostasis), fever, respiratory infections, digestive complaints

results.title_inferred: Echinacea — 13 nations, the Plains immune plant
results.original_text: [Echinacea angustifolia / E. purpurea — documented by Moerman across 13 Great Plains nations]
results.translation: Echinacea (Echinacea angustifolia primarily) is documented across 13 Great Plains nations including the Comanche, Cheyenne, Dakota, Kiowa, and Omaha. Traditional applications: toothache, sore throat, snake bite, infectious conditions, and as a general strengthener. The Comanche used it for toothache; the Cheyenne for sore throat; the Dakota for infections. The shared immune-strengthening application across independent nations led to the development of one of the world's best-selling herbal supplements. This entry adds specificity to the existing archive entry (MMA-025) with Moerman's documentation.
results.preparation: Root chewed or decocted for toothache; root tea for infections; root applied topically for snake bite
results.condition: Immune support, toothache, sore throat, infections, snake bite

results.original_text: [Monarda fistulosa — documented across 21 nations by Moerman]
results.translation: Wild bergamot (Monarda fistulosa) is documented across 21 North American nations including Blackfoot, Cheyenne, Cherokee, Iroquois, Ojibwe, and Potawatomi. Applications: respiratory infections, fever, digestive complaints, and topical antiseptic. The Blackfoot used it for respiratory infections; the Iroquois for fever; multiple nations used it as a wound antiseptic. Modern research: thymol and carvacrol — the same compounds that validate oregano and thyme in the Mediterranean and Andean traditions — are the primary active compounds. This is the North American parallel to Mediterranean oregano, an independent pharmacological discovery of the same thymol/carvacrol antimicrobial class.
results.preparation: Fresh or dried leaves decocted for internal use; leaves applied topically for antiseptic; steam inhalation
results.condition: Respiratory infections, fever, digestive complaints, wound antiseptic

results.title_inferred: Sage (Salvia / Artemisia) — 19 nations, the ceremonial-medical plant
results.original_text: [Salvia species and Artemisia species — documented across 19+ nations]
results.translation: Multiple sage species (Salvia and Artemisia) are documented across 19+ nations for both medicinal and ceremonial purposes — the distinction between them being culturally variable. White sage (Salvia apiana) is used for purification ceremonies across Southwest nations; common sage (Salvia officinalis) for digestive and respiratory conditions across Eastern nations; sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata) across Great Basin nations for fever and respiratory. The medicinal applications — antimicrobial, digestive, respiratory — are consistent across all sage species and connect to Mediterranean sage (Salvia officinalis) in the Greek/Roman tradition for the same applications.

results.title_inferred: Goldenseal — berberine in the Eastern Woodlands
results.original_text: [Hydrastis canadensis — Cherokee, Iroquois, Ojibwe documentation in Moerman]
results.translation: Goldenseal (Hydrastis canadensis) is documented by Cherokee, Iroquois, and Ojibwe nations for: eye infections, mouth conditions, digestive complaints, and as a general antimicrobial. The Cherokee specifically used goldenseal root tea for eye inflammation — the same application as Coptis/goldthread (MMA-068) documented in North American and Tibetan traditions. Both plants share berberine as the primary active compound — the same compound now studied for Type 2 diabetes (comparable to metformin in RCTs). This entry extends the berberine convergence to include goldenseal as a distinct North American species alongside Coptis trifolia, creating a documented five-species, five-tradition berberine convergence.
results.preparation: Root tea for eye infections (eyewash); root decoction for internal conditions; root powder applied topically
results.condition: Eye infections, mouth conditions, digestive complaints, skin infections

results.title_inferred: Sweetgrass — the ceremonial medicine of the Plains
results.translation: Sweetgrass (Hierochloe odorata) — documented across Plains and Eastern Woodland nations for both ceremonial and medicinal use. The Blackfoot used it for colds and coughs; the Ojibwe for ceremonial smudging and respiratory conditions; the Lakota for wound healing. Sweetgrass smoke was documented as an antimicrobial fumigation — consistent with the documented antimicrobial activity of coumarin (the compound responsible for sweetgrass's distinctive vanilla-hay scent). This connects to the archive's documentation of Tonka bean (coumarin source) in South American traditions — an independent discovery of the same antimicrobial compound from a different plant.

results.title_inferred: Willow bark — pre-aspirin pain medicine across North America
results.original_text: [Salix species — documented across multiple nations for pain and fever]
results.translation: Willow bark (Salix species) is documented across multiple North American nations for pain and fever — the same application documented in Egyptian, Greek/Roman, and Celtic/Norse traditions. The Ojibwe used willow bark tea for headache and fever; the Iroquois for joint pain; the Cherokee for fever. This multi-continental convergence for willow — pain and fever across Egyptian, Greek/Roman, Celtic, and North American independent traditions — is validated by salicin/salicylic acid pharmacology, and represents one of the most geographically extensive convergences in the archive. Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) was synthesized from salicylic acid in 1897.
results.preparation: Inner bark decocted in water for tea; bark chewed directly
results.condition: Headache, fever, joint pain, general pain — validated by salicin mechanism
